name: antibiotic_stockpile_export_or_local
premise: A resistant bacterial outbreak spikes ICU admissions. The country has a stockpile of a last-line antibiotic that can cover only three weeks of projected need. A neighboring state requests emergency doses for a prison outbreak and warns that uncontrolled spread will cross the border. Domestic hospitals are already delaying surgeries and clinicians fear running out. A shipping delay means new supplies cannot arrive for at least a month. Parliament demands that no doses leave the country; border towns and supply chains are interconnected. The minister must decide whether to export a portion under treaty obligations or keep all doses domestically. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Public Health.

agents.name: Minister Leilani Koa
agents.identity: I am the health minister managing a limited antibiotic stockpile during a resistant outbreak.
agents.goal: I want to protect my population while cooperating internationally without creating a tragedy of hoarding.

agents.name: WHO Liaison Ravi Singh
agents.identity: I am the international liaison coordinating equitable distribution to prevent regional collapse and mutation spread.
agents.goal: I want allocations that reduce overall harm, because uncontrolled spread elsewhere will return.

choices: I will export a defined portion with strict monitoring and mutual commitments, accepting domestic risk to reduce regional spread and long-term harm.
I will keep the stockpile entirely domestic and offer technical support instead, accepting regional spread risk to protect immediate national capacity.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Stakeholders are demanding a clear decision and the trade-offs are real. Minister Leilani Koa must choose between two defensible actions with different harms. What does Minister Leilani Koa do?
